---
title: "Chapter 08 — 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 07 · 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels](ch07.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [09 · 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning →](ch09.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Decision trees are among the most intuitive models in machine learning: they ask a sequence of yes/no questions about the data and arrive at an answer, exactly the way a person might diagnose a problem by elimination. On their own, single trees are easy to read but fragile. The real power comes from **ensembles** — combining many trees so their individual mistakes cancel out. This chapter sits inside classical (non-neural) ML, and the ensemble methods it describes, especially gradient boosting, remain the dominant approach for tabular data to this day.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Classical supervised learning · used for prediction on structured/tabular data · the one key idea is that combining many weak, decorrelated trees beats any single tuned tree.
:::

## 8.1 — How a decision tree splits data

Imagine sorting a pile of fruit. You first ask "is it round?", then "is it bigger than a fist?", then "is it red?" — each question slices the pile into more uniform sub-piles until each pile is essentially one fruit. A **decision tree** does exactly this: it recursively partitions the feature space with axis-aligned cuts, and each **leaf** node holds a prediction. The internal nodes are the questions; the branches are the answers; the leaves are the verdicts.

Training is **greedy recursive partitioning**. At each node the algorithm scans every feature and every candidate threshold, picks the single split that most improves a purity measure, then recurses on each child. It never reconsiders earlier splits — that is what "greedy" means. Finding the globally optimal tree is NP-hard, so this one-step-at-a-time heuristic is what everyone actually uses; it is fast and usually good enough.

The key question is how to measure "improvement" — how to score a candidate split. The idea is *purity*: a good split produces children that are more uniform (closer to a single class) than their parent. Two impurity measures dominate classification.

**Entropy** captures how mixed a node is, measured in bits:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{c} p_c \log_2 p_c$$

where $p_c$ is the fraction of class $c$ in node $S$. A pure node (all one class) has entropy 0; a 50/50 two-class split has entropy 1 (maximally uncertain). **Information gain** is then the entropy you remove by splitting — the parent's entropy minus the weighted average entropy of the children:

$$IG = H(\text{parent}) - \sum_{k}\frac{|S_k|}{|S|}H(S_k)$$

The split with the largest information gain wins.

**Gini impurity** measures the chance of mislabeling a random sample if you guessed labels by drawing from the node's class distribution:

$$G(S) = 1 - \sum_c p_c^2$$

It runs from 0 (pure) up to $1 - 1/C$ for $C$ equally likely classes. Gini and entropy almost always pick similar splits; Gini is marginally cheaper (no logarithm) and is the default in most libraries. Use whichever you like — the difference rarely matters in practice.

Here is the splitting heuristic from scratch, scoring every candidate cut and keeping the purest:

```python
import numpy as np

def gini(y):
    # 1 - sum of squared class proportions
    _, counts = np.unique(y, return_counts=True)
    p = counts / counts.sum()
    return 1 - (p**2).sum()

def best_split(X, y):
    best = (None, None, np.inf)  # (feature, threshold, weighted impurity)
    n = len(y)
    for f in range(X.shape[1]):                 # scan every feature
        for thr in np.unique(X[:, f]):          # ...and every candidate cut
            left = X[:, f] <= thr
            if left.sum() == 0 or left.sum() == n:
                continue                        # skip cuts that split nothing
            # weighted child impurity — lower is better
            imp = (left.sum()*gini(y[left]) + (~left).sum()*gini(y[~left])) / n
            if imp < best[2]:
                best = (f, thr, imp)
    return best

if __name__ == "__main__":
    X = np.array([[1.0],[2.0],[8.0],[9.0]])
    y = np.array([0,0,1,1])
    f, thr, imp = best_split(X, y)
    assert imp == 0.0 and thr == 2.0  # perfect split between 2 and 8
    print("split feature", f, "at <=", thr)
```

For **regression trees**, there are no classes, so impurity is replaced by variance (equivalently the sum of squared errors within a node), and each leaf predicts the **mean** of the training targets that land in it. The chosen split is the one that most reduces total within-node variance — the regression echo of information gain.

Because the cuts are axis-aligned, the model that a tree carves out of the feature space is a set of rectangular boxes, each labeled with one prediction:

<svg viewBox="0 0 220 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="240">
  <rect x="30" y="10" width="170" height="130" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="110" y1="10" x2="110" y2="140" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="110" y1="80" x2="200" y2="80" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="60" y="80" font-size="12" fill="currentColor">A</text>
  <text x="150" y="48" font-size="12" fill="currentColor">B</text>
  <text x="150" y="115" font-size="12" fill="currentColor">C</text>
  <text x="105" y="153" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">x₁ split</text>
  <text x="2" y="84" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">x₂ split</text>
</svg>

A finished classifier reads like a flowchart from root to leaf:

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A["petal length < 2.5?"] -->|yes| B["Setosa"]
    A -->|no| C["petal width < 1.8?"]
    C -->|yes| D["Versicolor"]
    C -->|no| E["Virginica"]
```

## 8.2 — Overfitting, pruning, and interpretability

A tree grown without limits will keep splitting until every leaf is pure — meaning, in the worst case, every training point gets its own tiny region. That tree memorizes the training set perfectly and generalizes terribly. This is the core weakness of trees: they are **high-variance** learners, exquisitely sensitive to small changes in the data. Reshuffle a few rows and the top split can flip, cascading into an entirely different tree.

There are two ways to control growth. **Pre-pruning** (early stopping) caps complexity *during* training — you forbid the tree from growing too far:

- **max depth** — the longest root-to-leaf path allowed.
- **min samples per leaf / per split** — refuse to create leaves backed by too few examples.
- **min impurity decrease** — only split if the purity gain clears a threshold, so marginal splits are skipped.

**Post-pruning** grows the full tree first, then collapses branches that do not pay their way. The standard form is **cost-complexity pruning**, which minimizes

$$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha\,|T|$$

where $R(T)$ is the tree's error on the data, $|T|$ is the number of leaves, and $\alpha \ge 0$ is a penalty charged per leaf. With $\alpha = 0$ you keep the full tree; as $\alpha$ grows, leaves that buy too little accuracy get pruned away and the tree shrinks. The best $\alpha$ is chosen by cross-validation. Post-pruning often generalizes a touch better than pre-pruning because it can let a weak split through if it enables a strong one beneath it — something early stopping, looking only one step ahead, would never discover.

The redeeming virtue of a single tree is **interpretability**. A shallow tree is a flowchart a domain expert can read, audit, and trust — every prediction traces back to a handful of human-readable rules ("petal length under 2.5, so Setosa"). Trees also need almost no preprocessing: no feature scaling (splits care only about order, not magnitude), native handling of mixed numeric and categorical features, and graceful behavior with missing values. That low-friction, glass-box nature is exactly why trees survive as the building block of the ensembles in the rest of this chapter, even though you would rarely ship one alone.

::: {.callout-warning}
A single deep decision tree is almost never the right production model. It is unstable — reshuffling a few rows can flip the top split and produce a completely different tree. Use single trees for explanation and exploration; use ensembles for accuracy.
:::

## 8.3 — Why ensembles help: the bias–variance lever

The reason ensembles work comes straight from the **bias–variance decomposition** (covered in depth in the evaluation chapter). A model's expected error splits into three parts: **bias** (systematic error from too-simple assumptions), **variance** (sensitivity to the particular training sample you happened to draw), and **irreducible noise** (the part no model can remove). Ensembles are tools for pushing down one of these two reducible terms.

Here is the crucial fact, and it is just statistics. If you average $M$ predictions that each have variance $\sigma^2$, and they are *perfectly independent*, the variance of the average shrinks to $\sigma^2 / M$ — it collapses toward zero as you add models. Real predictions from real models are not independent, though; they tend to make the same mistakes, with some average pairwise correlation $\rho$. Accounting for that correlation, the variance of the averaged prediction is:

$$\rho\,\sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{M}\,\sigma^2$$

Read that formula carefully — it is the whole theory of ensembles in one line. The second term vanishes as you add more models $M$, but the first term, $\rho\sigma^2$, does not: it is a floor set entirely by how correlated the members are. So an ensemble can help in exactly two ways: average **more** models (drives the second term down), or make them **less correlated** (drives the floor down). This is why every good ensemble method works hard to *decorrelate* its members — independence is the resource being spent.

That same split into bias and variance explains why there are two great families of tree ensembles, attacking the two reducible terms from opposite directions:

- **Bagging** attacks **variance**: it averages many low-bias, high-variance deep trees so their independent noise cancels, while their shared signal survives.
- **Boosting** attacks **bias**: it sequentially adds shallow, high-bias trees that each correct the leftover error of the ensemble so far, building a strong learner out of weak ones.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    E["Ensemble methods"] --> B["Bagging — parallel, cuts variance"]
    E --> O["Boosting — sequential, cuts bias"]
    B --> RF["Random Forest"]
    O --> AB["AdaBoost"]
    O --> GB["Gradient boosting → XGBoost / LightGBM / CatBoost"]
```

## 8.4 — Bagging and Random Forests

**Bagging** ("bootstrap aggregating") is the direct application of the variance formula. A **bootstrap sample** is a dataset of the same size as the original, drawn *with replacement* — so some rows appear several times, and (it works out to) about 37% of rows are left out of any given sample. You train one tree per bootstrap sample and average their predictions (or take a majority vote, for classification). Each tree sees a slightly different world, so their errors are partly independent, and averaging cancels the part that disagrees.

That handles the second term of the variance formula. To also lower the correlation floor $\rho$, the **Random Forest** adds one more trick — **feature randomness**. At every split, instead of considering all $p$ features, each tree may only choose from a fresh random subset (commonly $\sqrt{p}$ features for classification, $p/3$ for regression). This stops every tree from latching onto the same one or two dominant features, which would otherwise make all the trees agree (high $\rho$). The forest deliberately weakens each tree a little to make the trees disagree more — and the variance formula says that trade is worth it.

```python
import numpy as np
# Sketch: the two sources of randomness in a Random Forest
def bootstrap_idx(n):
    return np.random.randint(0, n, size=n)        # rows: sample WITH replacement

def feature_subset(p):
    k = max(1, int(np.sqrt(p)))                   # features: ~sqrt(p) per split
    return np.random.choice(p, size=k, replace=False)

# oob_mask: rows NOT drawn in a given bootstrap — a free validation set
def oob_mask(idx, n):
    inbag = np.zeros(n, dtype=bool)
    inbag[idx] = True
    return ~inbag                                 # ~37% of rows on average
```

Random Forests come with two practical gifts that fall out of the bootstrap for free:

- **Out-of-bag (OOB) error.** Each tree was trained without ~37% of the rows; predict those left-out rows with *that* tree, aggregate across all trees, and you get an unbiased generalization estimate at no extra cost — often no separate validation split is needed.
- **Feature importance.** Sum the impurity decrease each feature contributes across all trees to rank features by usefulness. More reliable is **permutation importance**: shuffle one feature's values on held-out data and measure how much accuracy drops — if breaking a feature barely hurts, it was not pulling much weight.

::: {.callout-warning}
Impurity-based feature importance is biased toward high-cardinality and continuous features, simply because they offer more places to split. When the ranking actually drives a decision, prefer permutation importance computed on held-out data.
:::

Random Forests are the great default: robust, hard to overfit (adding trees never increases variance, it only helps the average), nearly tuning-free, and trivially parallel since every tree trains independently.

## 8.5 — Boosting: AdaBoost and gradient boosting

Boosting takes the opposite stance from bagging. Instead of building independent trees in parallel and averaging, it builds them **sequentially**, each new tree focused on the mistakes of the ensemble so far. A single shallow tree — a "**weak learner**," often just a *stump* (one split) — has high bias and is barely better than guessing. Chain many together, each cleaning up its predecessor's errors, and they compound into a strong, low-bias model. Where bagging is a committee voting at once, boosting is a relay race.

**AdaBoost** (Adaptive Boosting) was the first practical version, and the cleanest way to see the idea. It keeps a weight on every training example, all equal at the start. After each weak learner trains, the examples it *misclassified* get their weights *increased*, so the next learner is pulled toward the hard cases; correctly handled examples fade in importance. The final prediction is a weighted vote in which more accurate learners count for more. Stubborn, hard-to-classify points keep climbing in weight until some learner finally gets them right.

**Gradient boosting** is the powerful generalization, and the one behind every modern library. The insight is to reframe boosting as **gradient descent in function space**: the ensemble is a sum of trees $F(x) = \sum_m \nu\, h_m(x)$, built up one tree at a time,

$$F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu\, h_m(x).$$

At each step you compute the negative gradient of the loss with respect to the current predictions — for squared-error loss these are exactly the **residuals** $y - F_{m-1}(x)$, the part you have gotten wrong so far — and fit the next tree $h_m$ to *those*. So each tree's job is literally to predict the remaining error. AdaBoost turns out to be just the special case of this recipe with an exponential loss. The **learning rate** $\nu$ (also called shrinkage, e.g. 0.1) scales each tree's contribution so the ensemble creeps toward the target in small, careful steps instead of overshooting.

```python
import numpy as np
# Gradient boosting for regression (squared-error loss), from scratch.
# fit_stump(X, target) returns a fitted shallow tree with a .predict(X) method.
def fit_gbm(X, y, fit_stump, n_rounds=50, lr=0.1):
    F = np.full(len(y), y.mean())     # start from the simplest guess: the mean
    trees = []
    for _ in range(n_rounds):
        residual = y - F              # negative gradient of squared-error loss
        h = fit_stump(X, residual)    # fit the next tree to what's left over
        F = F + lr * h.predict(X)     # take a small, shrunken step
        trees.append(h)
    return F, trees                   # final prediction = mean + lr*sum(trees)
# Each round nudges F a little closer to y by chasing the current residual.
```

The learning-rate tradeoff is classic: a smaller $\nu$ generalizes better but needs more rounds (trees) to reach the same fit, so $\nu$ and the number of rounds are tuned together — typically by fixing a small $\nu$ and using **early stopping** on a validation set to decide when to quit adding trees.

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb for the trees *inside* a boosting loop: keep them **shallow** (depth 3–8). Boosting drives down bias by stacking many trees, so each one should be a weak, low-variance learner. Deep trees in a boosting loop have low bias already and just overfit fast — the opposite of what you want here, and the opposite of the deep trees you want in a Random Forest.
:::

## 8.6 — Why XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost dominate tabular data

Plain gradient boosting is slow to train and overfits without care. The modern libraries took the same core algorithm and engineered it into the workhorse of applied ML — these are the models that win the majority of Kaggle competitions on structured data and quietly run countless production pipelines. They differ mostly in the engineering, not the math.

**XGBoost** added a **regularized objective** — explicitly penalizing the number of leaves and the magnitude of the leaf values — so the trees stay modest. It also uses a second-order (Newton) step, exploiting both the gradient *and* the **Hessian** (curvature) of the loss for sharper updates, and brought efficient handling of sparse and missing data plus cache-aware split finding.

**LightGBM** optimized for speed and scale with two ideas. **Histogram-based** splitting bins each continuous feature into ~256 buckets, so finding the best split scans a few hundred bins instead of every raw value. **Leaf-wise** growth splits whichever single leaf reduces the loss most (best-first), rather than expanding the tree level by level — which reaches lower loss with fewer trees, at the cost of needing depth limits to stay safe.

**CatBoost** focused on **categorical features** and a subtle leakage trap. Naively encoding a category by its mean target ("target encoding") leaks the label into the feature; CatBoost's **ordered target encoding** and **ordered boosting** compute those statistics using only rows seen *earlier* in a permutation, removing the bias. It handles categoricals natively and ships with strong defaults that often perform well with little tuning.

| Library | Tree growth | Categorical handling | Headline strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| XGBoost | level-wise (depth) | manual encoding | regularization, battle-tested, accurate |
| LightGBM | leaf-wise (best-first) | native, integer-coded | fastest on large datasets |
| CatBoost | symmetric (oblivious) | native, ordered target encoding | best out-of-box, leakage-safe categoricals |

Why do these beat deep learning on tables? Tabular data is heterogeneous (each column a different unit and meaning), often modestly sized, and full of informative axis-aligned thresholds — exactly the structure trees exploit and that neural nets struggle to learn without enormous data. For most spreadsheet-shaped problems, gradient-boosted trees are the strongest baseline you can reach for, and frequently the final model too.

## 8.7 — Stacking and blending

Bagging and boosting combine many copies of the *same* kind of model. **Stacking** (stacked generalization) instead combines *different* model types — say a random forest, a gradient-boosting model, and a logistic regression — and, crucially, *learns how* to combine them with another model rather than just averaging.

The structure has two levels. **Base learners** (level-0) each make predictions on the input. A **meta-learner** (level-1) then takes those predictions as its input features and produces the final answer. The meta-learner can discover, for instance, that one base model is reliable in some regions of the data and another is better elsewhere, and weight them accordingly.

The critical detail is avoiding leakage. The meta-learner must train on **out-of-fold** predictions: you generate each base model's prediction for a row using a copy of that model that never saw the row, exactly as in cross-validation. If you instead train the meta-learner on predictions the base models made about their *own* training rows, it will learn to trust an overfit signal that evaporates at test time — and the whole stack quietly fails in production.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    X[Input] --> M1[Random Forest]
    X --> M2[Gradient Boosting]
    X --> M3[Logistic Reg.]
    M1 --> Meta[Meta-learner]
    M2 --> Meta
    M3 --> Meta
    Meta --> Y[Final prediction]
```

**Blending** is the simpler cousin: hold out a single validation set, train the base models on the rest, and fit the meta-learner on their predictions over that one held-out set. It is easier to implement and leak-proof by construction, but it uses less data for the meta-learner, so it is slightly less robust than full out-of-fold stacking.

::: {.callout-tip}
Diversity is the fuel of stacking — recall the $\rho\sigma^2$ floor from 8.3. Stacking models that all make the *same* errors gains nothing; the win comes from combining learners whose mistakes are uncorrelated. And keep the meta-learner simple: a plain average or a linear model usually beats a fancy one, which tends to overfit the handful of base predictions.
:::

## 8.8 — Bagging vs boosting: a side-by-side comparison

The two ensemble families answer different problems, and knowing which lever you are pulling — variance or bias — tells you which to reach for and how to tune it.

| Aspect | Bagging (e.g. Random Forest) | Boosting (e.g. XGBoost) |
|---|---|---|
| Primarily reduces | Variance | Bias |
| Tree construction | Parallel, independent | Sequential, dependent |
| Base learner | Deep, low-bias trees | Shallow, high-bias trees |
| Sample focus | Bootstrap resamples | Re-weight / fit residuals of errors |
| Overfitting risk | Low; more trees rarely hurts | Higher; too many trees can overfit |
| Parallelizable | Yes (trees independent) | Limited (each tree needs the last) |
| Tuning effort | Low, forgiving defaults | Higher; learning rate × rounds × depth |
| Typical accuracy ceiling | Strong | Usually higher when well-tuned |

The practical workflow follows from the table. Start with a **Random Forest** as a robust, low-effort baseline to see what accuracy is achievable with almost no tuning. If you need to squeeze out more and can afford the tuning budget, move to **gradient boosting** (LightGBM or CatBoost are excellent first choices). Reach for **stacking** only at the very end, when small gains genuinely matter and you already have several strong, diverse models to combine.

## 8.9 — Key takeaways

- A decision tree recursively splits the feature space with greedy, axis-aligned cuts to maximize node purity (Gini/entropy for classification, variance for regression); leaves predict the majority class or the mean target.
- Single trees are interpretable and preprocessing-light but high-variance and unstable; control them with pre-pruning (max depth, min samples) or cost-complexity post-pruning.
- The ensemble variance formula $\rho\sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{M}\sigma^2$ is the whole story: add more models *and* decorrelate them, because $\rho\sigma^2$ is a floor you cannot average away.
- **Bagging / Random Forests** reduce *variance* by averaging deep trees over bootstrap samples plus per-split feature randomness; OOB error and feature importance come for free.
- **Boosting** reduces *bias* by sequentially fitting shallow trees to the residuals (negative gradient) of the ensemble so far, scaled by a small learning rate, with early stopping to pick the number of trees.
- **XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost** dominate tabular ML through regularization, histogram/leaf-wise growth, and leakage-safe native categorical handling — usually beating neural nets on spreadsheet-shaped data.
- **Stacking** combines diverse model *types* via a meta-learner trained on out-of-fold predictions; diversity, not raw individual strength, drives the gain.

## 8.10 — See also

- **Chapter 05 — Linear & Logistic Models**: the linear baselines often used as stacking base or meta-learners.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection**: the bias–variance decomposition, cross-validation, and out-of-fold mechanics in depth.
- **Chapter 04 — Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering**: encoding categoricals and handling missing values that tree libraries partly automate.
- **Chapter 09 — Clustering & Unsupervised Learning**: the unsupervised counterpart to the supervised partitioning seen here.
- **Chapter 36 — Time Series Analysis & Forecasting**: where gradient-boosted trees are now a leading method on tabular forecasting features.
- **Chapter 44 — Explainable AI & Interpretability**: SHAP and permutation importance for opening up tree ensembles.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 07 · 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels](ch07.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [09 · 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning →](ch09.html)
